back: A concurrency control protocol that ensures conflict-serializable schedules by dividing transaction execution into two phases: a growing phase (locks can be acquired but not released) and a shrinking phase (locks can be released but not acquired).
The first phase where a transaction can acquire all necessary locks but cannot release any lock. The transaction's lock count only increases during this phase.
The second phase where a transaction can release locks but cannot acquire any new locks. Once the first lock is released, the transaction enters this phase.
A lock that allows a transaction to read data but not modify it. Multiple transactions can hold shared locks on the same data item simultaneously.
A lock that allows both reading and writing of data. Only one transaction can hold an exclusive lock on a data item at any time, and no other lock (shared or exclusive) can be granted.
A situation where two or more transactions are waiting indefinitely for locks held by each other. Neither can proceed until the other releases its lock.
A situation where a transaction waits indefinitely for a lock because other transactions are repeatedly granted the lock ahead of it.
A variant that holds exclusive locks until the transaction commits or aborts, ensuring no cascading rollbacks. Shared locks can be released earlier.
A stricter variant that holds all locks (both shared and exclusive) until the transaction commits or aborts, preventing any cascading rollbacks.
A 2PL variant that acquires all locks needed by a transaction before the transaction begins execution, preventing deadlocks but requiring lock anticipation.
A table showing which lock types can be granted when another lock is already held. S-lock is compatible with S-lock; X-lock is incompatible with both S-lock and X-lock.
A problem where the abort of one transaction requires the rollback of other transactions that have read uncommitted data from the aborted transaction.
The point in the growing phase when a transaction acquires its final lock. The serializability order of transactions is determined by their lock points.
An alternative to 2PL that uses transaction timestamps rather than locks to ensure serializability. Transactions are ordered by their start times.
A modification to timestamp ordering that allows certain write operations to be ignored if they would be overwritten by a later timestamp, improving concurrency.
